subUnits.title: 10.1 Cell Reaction to Environment and Types of Responses
notes: Irritability:
Definition:
Irritability (or sensitivity) is the ability of living organisms to detect and respond to changes (stimuli) in their internal or external environment.
Importance of Irritability:
• Enables organisms to detect and avoid danger
• Helps in finding food, mates, and suitable habitats
• Allows adaptation to changing environmental conditions
• Essential for survival and reproduction
• Nigerian context: Irritability helps Nigerian crops respond to drought, pests, and other environmental stresses
Stimuli and Responses:
Stimulus:
• A change in the environment that triggers a response
• Examples: Light, temperature, touch, chemicals, gravity, sound
Response:
• The reaction of an organism to a stimulus
• Can be immediate or delayed, simple or complex
• Examples: Plant bending toward light, animal running from danger
Types of Responses:
1. Nastic Responses:
• Definition: Non-directional responses to stimuli
• Response is independent of stimulus direction
• Usually rapid and reversible
• Examples:
a) Nyctinasty: Sleep movements in response to light/dark cycles
- Example: Mimosa pudica leaves folding at night
- Nigerian example: Mimosa pudica (sensitive plant) in Nigerian gardens
b) Thigmonasty: Response to touch
- Example: Mimosa pudica leaves folding when touched
- Mechanism: Rapid loss of turgor in pulvini (motor organs)
- Advantage: May deter herbivores or reduce water loss
- Nigerian example: Mimosa pudica common in Nigerian roadsides and farms
c) Photonasty: Response to light intensity
- Example: Flowers opening in light, closing in dark
- Nigerian example: Morning glory flowers opening at dawn
2. Tropic Responses:
• Definition: Directional growth responses to stimuli
• Response direction depends on stimulus direction
• Usually slow and irreversible (growth-based)
• Examples:
a) Phototropism: Response to light
- Positive: Shoots grow toward light
- Negative: Roots grow away from light
- Mechanism: Auxin redistribution causes differential cell elongation
- Nigerian example: Maize seedlings bending toward light in shaded conditions
b) Geotropism (Gravitropism): Response to gravity
- Positive: Roots grow downward
- Negative: Shoots grow upward
- Mechanism: Statoliths (starch grains) settle in response to gravity
- Nigerian context: Understanding geotropism helps in planting crops with correct root-shoot orientation
c) Hydrotropism: Response to water
- Roots grow toward moisture sources
- Advantage: Enhances water uptake in dry soils
- Nigerian example: Crop roots growing toward irrigation channels or water tables
d) Thigmotropism: Response to touch
- Example: Tendrils coiling around supports
- Mechanism: Differential growth on contacted vs non-contacted sides
- Nigerian example: Yam tendrils coiling around stakes for support
3. Taxis:
• Definition: Directional movement of whole organisms toward or away from stimuli
• Occurs in motile organisms (animals, some protists)
• Examples:
a) Phototaxis: Movement in response to light
- Positive: Euglena moving toward light for photosynthesis
- Negative: Cockroaches moving away from light
- Nigerian example: Mosquitoes attracted to light at night
b) Chemotaxis: Movement in response to chemicals
- Positive: Sperm moving toward egg chemicals
- Negative: Bacteria moving away from toxins
- Nigerian context: Chemotaxis important in immune response to infections
c) Geotaxis: Movement in response to gravity
- Example: Earthworms moving downward in soil
- Nigerian example: Earthworms improving soil structure in Nigerian farms
Positive and Negative Responses:
Positive Response:
• Movement or growth toward the stimulus
• Examples: Shoots growing toward light (positive phototropism), roots growing toward water (positive hydrotropism)
• Advantage: Helps organisms obtain resources (light, water, nutrients)
Negative Response:
• Movement or growth away from the stimulus
• Examples: Roots growing away from light (negative phototropism), cockroaches running from light
• Advantage: Helps organisms avoid harmful conditions
Mechanisms of Irritability:
In Plants:
• Hormonal control (auxins, gibberellins, etc.)
• Turgor changes in motor cells (pulvini)
• Differential growth rates
• Nigerian example: Auxin redistribution in cassava shoots responding to light
In Animals:
• Nervous system: Rapid electrical signaling
• Endocrine system: Slower hormonal signaling
• Sensory organs: Detect specific stimuli
• Nigerian context: Human nervous system responding to environmental changes
Adaptations for Irritability:
1. In Plants:
• Stomata: Open/close in response to light, CO₂, water
• Tendrils: Coil around supports for climbing
• Leaf movements: Reduce water loss or herbivory
• Nigerian example: Drought-adapted plants with rapid stomatal responses
2. In Animals:
• Sensory organs: Eyes, ears, nose, skin receptors
• Nervous system: Rapid signal transmission
• Behavioral adaptations: Migration, hibernation, camouflage
• Nigerian example: Birds migrating in response to seasonal changes
Practical Activities:
1. Demonstration of Nastic Response:
• Teacher demonstrates nastic response using Mimosa pudica plant
• Students observe and record leaf folding when touched
• Discussion: What is the advantage of this response?
2. Demonstration of Tropic Responses:
• Teacher performs experiments to show response to light and earth using plant shoot and plant root
• a) Phototropic response of shoots: Place seedlings in unilateral light
• b) Geotropic response of roots: Place seedlings horizontally
• c) Geotropic response of shoots: Observe upward growth
• d) Phototactic response in earthworms: Observe movement away from light
• Students record observations and draw conclusions
3. Observing Taxis:
• Place earthworms on moist paper with light on one side
• Observe movement away from light (negative phototaxis)
• Nigerian context: Relate to soil organisms avoiding harmful conditions
Common Misconceptions:
• 'Plants don't respond to stimuli': Plants show various responses (tropisms, nastic movements)
• 'All responses are immediate': Some responses (tropisms) are slow growth-based
• 'Taxis only occurs in animals': Some protists also show taxis
• 'Irritability is the same as movement': Irritability is the ability to respond; movement is one type of response
Nigerian Context:
• Understanding irritability helps Nigerian farmers manage crop responses to environmental stresses
• Knowledge of plant tropisms important for optimal planting and training of crops
• Animal irritability knowledge useful in pest control and livestock management
• Irritability concepts applied in Nigerian agriculture, health, and environmental management
Conclusion:
• Irritability is a fundamental characteristic of living organisms
• Responses can be nastic (non-directional), tropic (directional growth), or taxis (directional movement)
• Positive and negative responses help organisms obtain resources and avoid harm
• Understanding irritability has applications in agriculture, pest control, and environmental management
• In Nigeria, this knowledge supports crop management, livestock care, and ecological conservation

quiz.q: Irritability is best defined as the ability to:
opts: Detect and respond to changes in the environment
quiz.explanation: Irritability (sensitivity) is the ability of organisms to detect stimuli and respond appropriately.

quiz.q: A nastic response is characterized by:
opts: Non-directional response independent of stimulus direction
quiz.explanation: Nastic responses are non-directional; the response occurs regardless of the direction from which the stimulus comes.

opts: Mimosa pudica leaves folding when touched
quiz.explanation: Thigmonasty is a nastic response to touch, as seen in Mimosa pudica leaf folding.

quiz.q: Phototropism in plant shoots is typically:
quiz.explanation: Shoots exhibit positive phototropism, growing toward light to maximize photosynthesis.
Roots exhibit positive geotropism, growing downward in response to gravity to anchor the plant and access water.

quiz.q: Taxis differs from tropism because taxis involves:
quiz.explanation: Taxis is directional movement of the whole organism, while tropism is directional growth response.

quiz.q: A Nigerian example of phototaxis is:
opts: Mosquitoes attracted to light at night
quiz.explanation: Mosquitoes showing positive phototaxis by moving toward light sources at night is common in Nigeria.
Phototropism occurs when auxin redistributes to the shaded side of shoots, causing cells there to elongate more.

quiz.q: Understanding irritability is important for Nigerian agriculture because it:
opts: Helps manage crop responses to environmental stresses and optimize growth
quiz.explanation: Knowledge of plant responses helps farmers manage crops under varying light, water, and touch conditions.

quiz.q: A common misconception about irritability is:
opts: Responses can be positive or negative
quiz.explanation: Plants do respond to stimuli through tropisms, nastic movements, and other mechanisms, contrary to the misconception.
